nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving and stagnant waters including swamps, ponds, ditches, flooded rice paddies, marshes, and sluggish river backwaters; often in muddy, oxygen-depleted conditions where air-breathing ability is essential

decorPreferences: caves and PVC pipe hides for refuge
tight-fitting lid — essential due to extreme escape risk

diet.notes: Opportunistic predator and scavenger. Accepts high-protein sinking pellets, carnivore sticks, frozen bloodworms, earthworms, and live or frozen feeder invertebrates. Also takes vegetable matter and algae wafers on occasion. Feeds predominantly at night; nocturnal feeding schedule is recommended. Live feeder fish should be avoided to prevent disease transmission and habituation to live prey only.

avoidWith: all tankmates — this species should be kept alone
other catfish (territorial and prone to fin-injuring conspecific attacks)
handler hands and arms (venomous pectoral spines cause severe envenomation)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase reference image of Heteropneustes fossilis showing typical eel-like body and pectoral spine structure.

summary: A large (up to 30 cm), eel-like South Asian catfish bearing medically significant venomous pectoral spines responsible for recorded human fatalities in fisheries. An obligate air-breather. Highly aggressive; specialist-only, single-specimen tank with strict handler-safety protocols required.
careNotes: The Stinging Catfish is a formidable, predatory species distributed widely across South and Southeast Asian wetlands — India, Bangladesh, Sri Lanka, Myanmar, Thailand, Cambodia, Laos, and Vietnam — where it inhabits hypoxic swamps, rice paddies, and slow river backwaters. Its most defining and dangerous characteristic is a pair of venomous pectoral spines equipped with venom glands; envenomation causes intense, immediate pain, local tissue necrosis, and systemic effects. Human fatalities have been recorded among South Asian fisherfolk handling H. fossilis without protection. Aquarists must use puncture-resistant gloves and dedicated net equipment whenever the tank requires maintenance; spines can penetrate standard fishkeeping gloves. H. fossilis is an obligate air-breather via a long, sac-like accessory respiratory organ extending from the gill chamber toward the tail, which supplements gill respiration in low-oxygen environments. The tank surface must remain permanently accessible — never cover with a solid airtight seal, and ensure the fish can reach air within a few centimetres of surfacing. Tanks must be escape-proofed: H. fossilis is an exceptionally strong, determined escape artist with elongated musculature capable of pushing open poorly weighted lids. All gaps, filter inlets, and overflows must be sealed. A species-only tank of no less than 300 L (150 cm footprint) is mandatory. The species is nocturnal and territorial; dim or red night lighting enables natural behaviour observation. Water quality must be maintained well — despite the species’ wild tolerance of hypoxic conditions, stable, clean water maximises lifespan and reduces stress-driven aggression. Although found in somewhat soft, acidic to mildly alkaline conditions in the wild, H. fossilis is moderately adaptable. Copper-containing medications must be avoided as catfishes are copper-sensitive.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity is rarely undertaken outside research institutions and specialist biotope keepers. Wild spawning occurs during the monsoon season (June–September) when water temperatures rise and flooding triggers reproductive behaviour. Males build shallow nests in marginal vegetation; females are approached and stimulated to spawn. Eggs are adhesive and scattered or laid in nest depressions. No paternal mouthbrooding occurs. Captive breeding requires a very large dedicated breeding tank (600 L+), a compatible pair (risky — pectoral spine injuries between individuals are common), and monsoon-simulation cues (rising temperature 26–28°C, increased water changes mimicking rain dilution, reduced lighting). Hormone injection (HCG or Ovaprim) has been used successfully in aquaculture settings in India and Bangladesh. Fry are small and require live artemia nauplii and microworms initially. Breeding difficulty is rated advanced and is not recommended for home aquarists.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Heteropneustes fossilis
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 30 cm), temperature range (22–28°C), pH (6.0–8.0), hardness, native range across South and Southeast Asia, habitat (stagnant/slow waters, rice paddies, swamps), air-breathing biology, venomous spine warning, diet (omnivore / opportunistic predator), aggression, and breeding information. Notes medically significant envenomation risk and recommends species-only housing.

additional.name: FishBase — Heteropneustes fossilis
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult TL (max reported 30 cm), taxonomy (Heteropneustidae, Siluriformes), country distribution (India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Nepal, Sri Lanka, Myanmar, Thailand, Cambodia, Laos, Vietnam), habitat ecology (freshwater, demersal, benthopelagic in muddy sluggish waters), air-breathing organ notes, lifespan data, and IUCN status (Least Concern). Confirms venomous pectoral spines.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Heteropneustes fossilis species profile
additional.notes: Confirmed care level (advanced/specialist), species-only tank requirement, escape risk (high), venomous spine severity, handler safety protocols, nocturnal feeding behaviour, and minimum tank recommendations (300 L+). Notes medical significance of sting in fisheries workers.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Stinging Catfish care overview
additional.notes: Supplementary trade perspective on availability, handling safety, and aquarium husbandry; confirmed omnivore diet, air-breathing requirement, escape risk, and specialist-only classification. additional.name: TFH Magazine — Venomous Freshwater Catfishes of Asia
additional.notes: Contextual source on venom biology of Heteropneustes and related genera; confirmed venom gland anatomy at base of pectoral spines, clinical envenomation presentations (severe pain, oedema, necrosis, systemic effects), recorded fatal cases in fisheries context, and recommended aquarium-handling precautions. Supplemented hobbyistAdvisory rationale.

fish.breedingNotes: Rarely bred in home aquaria. Requires monsoon-simulation cues (rising temperature, increased water changes), 600 L+ breeding tank, and a compatible pair. Hormone induction used in Asian aquaculture. Significant injury risk between pairing adults due to pectoral spines.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Medically significant venomous pectoral spines causing severe envenomation in handler. Air-breather. ~30cm. Aggressive. Single-fish species-only tank with handler-safety protocols.
